english_explanations: A mathematical object that has both magnitude and direction, or an ordered list of numbers in a vector space.
example_sentences: The velocity of the particle is represented by a vector pointing northeast.

english_explanations: A single numerical value without direction, used to scale vectors or matrices.
example_sentences: Multiplying a matrix by a scalar scales every entry in the matrix uniformly.

english_explanations: A rectangular array of numbers, symbols, or expressions arranged in rows and columns.
example_sentences: The matrix has three rows and four columns, so it is a 3×4 matrix.

english_explanations: A collection of vectors that is closed under addition and scalar multiplication, satisfying specific axioms.
example_sentences: The set of all polynomials of degree at most two forms a vector space.

english_explanations: An expression constructed by multiplying each vector by a scalar and adding the results together.
example_sentences: Any vector in the plane can be written as a linear combination of the standard basis vectors.

english_explanations: The set of all possible linear combinations of a given set of vectors.
example_sentences: The span of two non-parallel vectors in R³ is a plane passing through the origin.

english_explanations: A linearly independent set of vectors that spans the entire vector space.
example_sentences: The standard basis for R² consists of the vectors (1,0) and (0,1).

english_explanations: The number of vectors in any basis for a vector space, representing the space's degrees of freedom.
example_sentences: The dimension of the solution space tells us how many free variables exist in the system.

english_explanations: A subset of a vector space that is itself a vector space under the same operations.
example_sentences: The set of all vectors whose third component is zero is a subspace of R³.

english_explanations: The set of all vectors that a linear transformation maps to the zero vector.
example_sentences: The kernel of the matrix consists of all vectors x such that Ax equals zero.

english_explanations: The vector space spanned by the columns of a matrix, representing all possible outputs of the linear transformation.
example_sentences: The column space of the matrix is the set of all vectors that can be written as Ax for some x.

english_explanations: The vector space spanned by the rows of a matrix.
example_sentences: The dimension of the row space equals the rank of the matrix.

english_explanations: A scalar λ such that there exists a nonzero vector v satisfying Av = λv for a square matrix A.
example_sentences: If a matrix has an eigenvalue of zero, then it is singular and not invertible.

english_explanations: A nonzero vector that only changes by a scalar factor when a linear transformation is applied to it.
example_sentences: An eigenvector of a rotation matrix points along the axis of rotation.

english_explanations: A scalar value computed from a square matrix that determines whether the matrix is invertible and describes geometric scaling.
example_sentences: The determinant of a 2×2 matrix ad − bc tells us the area scaling factor of the associated linear map.

english_explanations: The sum of the diagonal entries of a square matrix, equal to the sum of its eigenvalues.
example_sentences: The trace of a matrix is invariant under similarity transformations.

english_explanations: The matrix obtained by swapping the rows and columns of the original matrix.
example_sentences: The transpose of a column vector is a row vector with the same entries.

english_explanations: A matrix that, when multiplied by the original square matrix, yields the identity matrix.
example_sentences: A matrix has an inverse if and only if its determinant is nonzero.

english_explanations: A square matrix with ones on the main diagonal and zeros elsewhere, acting as the multiplicative identity.
example_sentences: Multiplying any matrix by the identity matrix of appropriate size leaves the matrix unchanged.

english_explanations: A matrix formed by appending the columns of two matrices, often used to represent a system of linear equations.
example_sentences: We solve the linear system by reducing its augmented matrix to row echelon form.

english_explanations: A matrix form where all nonzero rows are above any rows of all zeros, and the leading coefficient of each row is to the right of the one above it.
example_sentences: Gaussian elimination transforms any matrix into row echelon form.

english_explanations: A row echelon form where each leading entry is 1 and is the only nonzero entry in its column.
example_sentences: Gauss-Jordan elimination produces the reduced row echelon form, from which the solution is immediate.

english_explanations: A property of a set of vectors where no vector can be written as a linear combination of the others.
example_sentences: The columns of an invertible matrix are linearly independent.

english_explanations: A binary operation that takes two vectors and returns a scalar, generalizing the notion of angle and length.
example_sentences: The inner product of two orthogonal vectors is zero.

english_explanations: A function that assigns a strictly positive length or size to each vector in a vector space.
example_sentences: The Euclidean norm of a vector is the square root of the sum of the squares of its components.

english_explanations: Describing two vectors whose inner product is zero, or geometrically perpendicular to each other.
example_sentences: Two vectors are orthogonal if and only if their dot product equals zero.

english_explanations: Describing a set of vectors that are mutually orthogonal and each have unit norm.
example_sentences: The columns of an orthogonal matrix form an orthonormal set.

english_explanations: A mapping between vector spaces that preserves vector addition and scalar multiplication.
example_sentences: Matrix multiplication represents a linear transformation from one Euclidean space to another.

english_explanations: The dimension of the column space of a matrix, or equivalently the number of linearly independent rows or columns.
example_sentences: The rank of a matrix equals the number of pivot positions in its row echelon form.

english_explanations: The dimension of the null space of a matrix, representing the number of free variables in the homogeneous system.
example_sentences: The rank-nullity theorem states that rank plus nullity equals the number of columns.

english_explanations: The determinant of a smaller square matrix obtained by deleting one row and one column from the original matrix.
example_sentences: To compute a 3×3 determinant, we expand along a row using its minors.

english_explanations: A minor multiplied by (−1)^(i+j), used in cofactor expansion and computing the adjugate matrix.
example_sentences: The cofactor of an entry is its minor with the appropriate sign based on its position.

english_explanations: The transpose of the cofactor matrix of a square matrix, used in computing the inverse.
example_sentences: The inverse of a matrix can be found by dividing its adjugate by its determinant.

english_explanations: A method for computing the determinant of a matrix by recursively expanding along a row or column using cofactors.
example_sentences: Laplace expansion along the first row reduces an n×n determinant to a sum of (n−1)×(n−1) determinants.

english_explanations: A formula for solving a system of linear equations using determinants, applicable when the coefficient matrix is invertible.
example_sentences: Cramer's rule expresses each variable as a ratio of two determinants.

english_explanations: Describing a system of linear equations where all constant terms are zero, i.e., Ax = 0.
example_sentences: A homogeneous system always has at least the trivial solution x = 0.

english_explanations: Describing a system of linear equations where the constant terms are not all zero, i.e., Ax = b with b ≠ 0.
example_sentences: The general solution to an inhomogeneous system is a particular solution plus the general solution of the homogeneous system.

english_explanations: Describing a system of linear equations that has at least one solution.
example_sentences: If the rank of the augmented matrix equals the rank of the coefficient matrix, the system is consistent.

english_explanations: Describing a system of linear equations that has no solution.
example_sentences: An overdetermined system with contradictory equations is inconsistent.

english_explanations: The first nonzero entry in a row of a matrix in row echelon form, used as a reference during elimination.
example_sentences: Each pivot position corresponds to a basic variable in the system.

english_explanations: A variable in a linear system that does not correspond to a pivot column, allowing infinitely many solutions.
example_sentences: Free variables can be assigned arbitrary values, determining the remaining variables.

english_explanations: A variable in a linear system that corresponds to a pivot column in the coefficient matrix.
example_sentences: Basic variables are determined once values are assigned to the free variables.

english_explanations: A matrix obtained by performing a single elementary row operation on the identity matrix.
example_sentences: Multiplying by an elementary matrix on the left performs the corresponding row operation.

english_explanations: One of three operations on a matrix: swapping rows, multiplying a row by a nonzero scalar, or adding a multiple of one row to another.
example_sentences: Elementary row operations preserve the solution set of a linear system.

english_explanations: A bijective function that rearranges the elements of a finite set, or the resulting arrangement.
example_sentences: Every permutation can be expressed as a product of transpositions.

english_explanations: A square matrix obtained by permuting the rows of an identity matrix, used to rearrange rows or columns.
example_sentences: Left-multiplying by a permutation matrix reorders the rows of the original matrix.

english_explanations: A matrix that is interpreted as having been broken into sections called blocks or submatrices.
example_sentences: Block matrix multiplication proceeds by treating each block as a single matrix entry.

english_explanations: The polynomial det(A − λI) whose roots are the eigenvalues of the square matrix A.
example_sentences: The characteristic polynomial of a 2×2 matrix is a quadratic equation in λ.

english_explanations: A theorem stating that every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation.
example_sentences: By the Cayley-Hamilton theorem, a matrix satisfies a polynomial equation of degree equal to its size.

english_explanations: A function of two vector arguments that is linear in each argument separately.
example_sentences: The dot product is the canonical example of a symmetric bilinear form on Euclidean space.

english_explanations: A homogeneous polynomial of degree two in several variables, often represented as xᵀAx.
example_sentences: The equation of a conic section can be written as a quadratic form in two variables.

english_explanations: Describing a symmetric matrix (or quadratic form) for which xᵀAx > 0 for all nonzero vectors x.
example_sentences: A positive definite matrix has all positive eigenvalues.

english_explanations: Describing a symmetric matrix for which xᵀAx ≥ 0 for all vectors x.
example_sentences: The covariance matrix of any random vector is always positive semidefinite.

english_explanations: Describing a symmetric matrix for which xᵀAx < 0 for all nonzero vectors x.
example_sentences: A negative definite quadratic form attains a strict global maximum at the origin.

english_explanations: Describing a symmetric matrix for which xᵀAx ≤ 0 for all vectors x.
example_sentences: The Hessian being negative semidefinite at a critical point indicates a possible local maximum.

english_explanations: Describing a symmetric matrix (or quadratic form) that takes both positive and negative values.
example_sentences: An indefinite quadratic form yields a saddle point at the origin.

english_explanations: The triple (p, q, r) counting positive, negative, and zero eigenvalues of a symmetric matrix, or p − q.
example_sentences: Sylvester's law of inertia states that the signature of a real symmetric matrix is preserved under congruence.

english_explanations: A factorization of a matrix into the product of a unitary matrix, a diagonal matrix of singular values, and another unitary matrix.
example_sentences: Singular value decomposition exists for any matrix, even if it is not square or diagonalizable.

english_explanations: A factorization of a matrix into a lower triangular matrix L and an upper triangular matrix U.
example_sentences: LU decomposition simplifies solving multiple linear systems with the same coefficient matrix.

english_explanations: A factorization of a matrix into an orthogonal matrix Q and an upper triangular matrix R.
example_sentences: QR decomposition is widely used in numerical algorithms for least squares problems.

english_explanations: A decomposition of a positive definite matrix into the product of a lower triangular matrix and its transpose.
example_sentences: Cholesky decomposition requires roughly half the computation of LU decomposition for symmetric positive definite matrices.

english_explanations: A theorem stating that a normal matrix can be diagonalized by a unitary matrix, with orthogonal eigenvectors.
example_sentences: The spectral theorem guarantees that every real symmetric matrix has an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.

english_explanations: The nonnegative square roots of the eigenvalues of AᵀA, appearing on the diagonal in singular value decomposition.
example_sentences: The largest singular value of a matrix equals its operator norm.

english_explanations: The square root of the sum of the absolute squares of all matrix entries, analogous to the Euclidean vector norm.
example_sentences: The Frobenius norm of a matrix equals the square root of the sum of the squares of its singular values.

english_explanations: A measure of how much the output of a function changes with respect to small changes in the input, often defined as the ratio of largest to smallest singular value.
example_sentences: A large condition number indicates that the matrix is ill-conditioned and numerical solutions may be unstable.

english_explanations: Describing a problem or matrix whose solution is highly sensitive to small changes in the input data.
example_sentences: Solving an ill-conditioned linear system can amplify rounding errors dramatically.

english_explanations: Describing a problem or matrix whose solution is not highly sensitive to small perturbations in the input.
example_sentences: A matrix with a condition number close to one is well-conditioned.

english_explanations: A method for finding the best approximate solution to an overdetermined system by minimizing the sum of squared residuals.
example_sentences: The least squares solution minimizes the Euclidean norm of the residual vector.

english_explanations: Describing a system of linear equations with more equations than unknowns, typically having no exact solution.
example_sentences: An overdetermined system is usually solved in the least squares sense.

english_explanations: Describing a system of linear equations with fewer equations than unknowns, typically having infinitely many solutions.
example_sentences: An underdetermined consistent system has at least one free variable.

english_explanations: To transform a matrix into a diagonal matrix by a similarity transformation using its eigenvectors.
example_sentences: A matrix is diagonalizable if it has enough linearly independent eigenvectors to form a basis.

english_explanations: Describing a square matrix that can be transformed into a diagonal matrix via a similarity transformation.
example_sentences: Not every matrix is diagonalizable; defective matrices lack a full set of eigenvectors.

english_explanations: A relation between two square matrices A and B such that B = P⁻¹AP for some invertible matrix P.
example_sentences: Similar matrices represent the same linear transformation under different bases.

english_explanations: The process of converting the coordinates of a vector or the matrix of a linear transformation from one basis to another.
example_sentences: A change of basis matrix converts coordinate vectors from the old basis to the new basis.

english_explanations: Describing a complex square matrix whose conjugate transpose is also its inverse, preserving the Hermitian inner product.
example_sentences: A unitary matrix has orthonormal columns with respect to the complex inner product.

english_explanations: Describing a complex square matrix that is equal to its own conjugate transpose.
example_sentences: The eigenvalues of a Hermitian matrix are always real.

english_explanations: A square matrix that is equal to its own transpose, with entries symmetric across the main diagonal.
example_sentences: Every real symmetric matrix is orthogonally diagonalizable.

english_explanations: The process of scaling a vector or function so that its norm becomes one, or adjusting data to a standard scale.
example_sentences: Normalization of eigenvectors ensures they have unit length.

english_explanations: An algorithm for solving systems of linear equations by systematically eliminating variables using elementary row operations.
example_sentences: Gaussian elimination transforms the augmented matrix into row echelon form to reveal the solution.

english_explanations: An extension of Gaussian elimination that further reduces the matrix to reduced row echelon form, yielding the solution directly.
example_sentences: Gauss-Jordan elimination continues the reduction process until the coefficient matrix becomes the identity.
